title: Seaborn Intro
content:
Seaborn Introduction

What is Seaborn?

Seaborn is a Python data visualization library based on matplotlib. It provides a high-level interface for drawing attractive and informative statistical graphics.

Why Use Seaborn?

Seaborn integrates closely with pandas data structures. Its plotting functions operate on dataframes and arrays containing whole datasets and internally perform the necessary semantic mapping and statistical aggregation to produce informative plots.

Example
import seaborn as sns
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Apply the default theme
sns.set_theme()

# Load an example dataset
tips = sns.load_dataset("tips")

# Create a visualization
sns.relplot(
    data=tips,
    x="total_bill", y="tip", col="time",
    hue="smoker", style="smoker", size="size",
)
plt.show()

title: Loading Datasets
content:
Loading Example Datasets

Built-in Datasets

Seaborn comes with a few built-in datasets that are very useful for learning and practicing data visualization. You can load them using load_dataset().

Example

Loading Data Example
import seaborn as sns
import pandas as pd

# List available datasets
# print(sns.get_dataset_names())

# Load the 'iris' dataset
iris = sns.load_dataset('iris')

print("First 5 rows of the Iris dataset:")
print(iris.head())

print("\nSummary statistics:")
print(iris.describe())

title: Relational Plots
content:
Relational Plots

Visualizing Statistical Relationships

The relplot() function is a figure-level function for drawing relational plots onto a FacetGrid. Under the hood, it uses scatterplot() or lineplot().

Example

Relplot Example
import seaborn as sns
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

fmri = sns.load_dataset("fmri")

# Plot the responses for different events and regions
sns.relplot(
    data=fmri, kind="line",
    x="timepoint", y="signal",
    hue="region", style="event",
)
plt.title('FMRI Signal over Time')
plt.show()

title: Distribution Plots
content:
Distribution Plots

Visualizing Distributions of Data

The displot() function provides access to several approaches for visualizing the univariate or bivariate distribution of data, such as histograms and KDE plots.

Example

Displot Example
import seaborn as sns
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

penguins = sns.load_dataset("penguins")

# Create a histogram with a KDE curve
sns.displot(
    data=penguins, x="flipper_length_mm", 
    hue="species", kde=True, 
    element="step", stat="density"
)
plt.title('Distribution of Flipper Lengths')
plt.show()

title: Categorical Plots
content:
Categorical Plots

Visualizing Categorical Data

The catplot() function is used to plot categorical data. You can easily switch between different representations like bar plots, box plots, violin plots, and swarm plots using the kind parameter.

Example

Catplot Example
import seaborn as sns
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

titanic = sns.load_dataset("titanic")

# Create a violin plot showing age distribution by class
sns.catplot(
    data=titanic, x="class", y="age", 
    hue="survived", kind="violin", 
    split=True, inner="quartile"
)
plt.title('Age Distribution by Passenger Class')
plt.show()

title: Regression Plots
content:
Regression Plots

Visualizing Linear Relationships

Seaborn makes it easy to visualize linear relationships in your data. The lmplot() function combines regplot() and FacetGrid to plot data and regression models across subsets.

Example

Lmplot Example
import seaborn as sns
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

tips = sns.load_dataset("tips")

# Plot a linear regression with confidence intervals
sns.lmplot(
    data=tips, x="total_bill", y="tip",
    hue="smoker", col="time", 
    height=5, aspect=1
)
plt.show()

title: Matrix Plots
content:
Matrix Plots

Exploring Multi-dimensional Data

Matrix plots like heatmap() and clustermap() are excellent ways to visualize data where both rows and columns represent meaningful variables, such as correlation matrices or pivot tables.

Example

Heatmap Example
import seaborn as sns
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

flights = sns.load_dataset("flights")
flights_pivot = flights.pivot(index="month", columns="year", values="passengers")

plt.figure(figsize=(10, 8))
sns.heatmap(flights_pivot, annot=True, fmt='d', cmap="YlGnBu")
plt.title('Monthly Passengers Over Time')
plt.show()

title: Multi-plot Grids
content:
Multi-plot Grids

Pairplot & Jointplot

Functions like pairplot() and jointplot() create multi-plot grids that let you explore the relationships between many variables simultaneously.

Example

Pairplot Example
import seaborn as sns
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

penguins = sns.load_dataset("penguins")

# Create a pariplot to view pairwise relationships
sns.pairplot(penguins, hue="species", corner=True)
plt.suptitle('Pairwise Relationships of Penguin Features', y=1.02)
plt.show()

title: Styling and Themes
content:
Styling and Themes

Controlling Figure Aesthetics

Seaborn makes it easy to control the look and feel of your plots using set_theme(), set_style(), and color palettes.

Example

Styling Example
import seaborn as sns
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np

# Set a darkgrid style and a specific color palette
sns.set_style("darkgrid")
sns.set_palette("husl")

data = np.random.normal(size=(20, 5)) + np.arange(5) / 2

sns.boxplot(data=data)
sns.despine(offset=10, trim=True)
plt.title('Custom Styled Boxplot')
plt.show()

title: KDE Plots
content:
KDE Plots (Kernel Density Estimate)

Visualizing Continuous Distributions

A kernel density estimate (KDE) plot is a method for visualizing the distribution of observations in a dataset, analogous to a histogram. KDE represents the data using a continuous probability density curve.

Example

KDE Plot Example
import seaborn as sns
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

tips = sns.load_dataset("tips")

sns.kdeplot(
    data=tips, x="total_bill", hue="time",
    fill=True, common_norm=False, palette="crest",
    alpha=.5, linewidth=0,
)
plt.title('KDE Plot of Total Bill by Time')
plt.show()

title: ECDF Plots
content:
ECDF Plots

Empirical Cumulative Distribution Functions

An ECDF plot represents the proportion or count of observations falling below each unique value in a dataset. It is highly useful for comparing distributions across different subsets.

Example

ECDF Plot Example
import seaborn as sns
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

penguins = sns.load_dataset("penguins")

sns.ecdfplot(data=penguins, x="flipper_length_mm", hue="species")
plt.title('ECDF Plot of Flipper Lengths')
plt.show()

title: Rug Plots
content:
Rug Plots

Plotting Marginal Distributions

A rug plot draws a small vertical tick at each observation to represent marginal distributions. It is often used in combination with other plots like scatterplots or KDEs.

Example

Rug Plot Example
import seaborn as sns
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

tips = sns.load_dataset("tips")

sns.scatterplot(data=tips, x="total_bill", y="tip")
sns.rugplot(data=tips, x="total_bill", y="tip", height=.05, color="red")
plt.title('Scatterplot with Rugplot')
plt.show()

title: Violin Plots
content:
Violin Plots

Combining Box and KDE Plots

Violin plots show the distribution of quantitative data across several levels of categorical variables. They feature a KDE on each side, making them more informative than basic box plots.

Example

Violin Plot Example
import seaborn as sns
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

tips = sns.load_dataset("tips")

sns.violinplot(data=tips, x="day", y="total_bill", hue="smoker", split=True, inner="quartile")
plt.title('Violin Plot: Total Bill by Day and Smoker')
plt.show()

title: Swarm and Strip Plots
content:
Swarm and Strip Plots

Visualizing Categorical Scatterplots

Strip plots draw a scatterplot where one variable is categorical. Swarm plots adjust the points along the categorical axis so that they don't overlap, providing a better view of the distribution.

Example

Swarm Plot Example
import seaborn as sns
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

tips = sns.load_dataset("tips")

sns.boxplot(data=tips, x="day", y="total_bill", color="lightgrey")
sns.swarmplot(data=tips, x="day", y="total_bill", size=4)
plt.title('Box Plot overlaid with Swarm Plot')
plt.show()

title: Point Plots
content:
Point Plots

Estimate Trends with Point Plots

Point plots show point estimates and confidence intervals using scatter plot glyphs. This is extremely useful to focus on one main trend across categorical grouping variables.

Example

Point Plot Example
import seaborn as sns
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

tips = sns.load_dataset("tips")

sns.pointplot(data=tips, x="time", y="total_bill", hue="smoker")
plt.title('Point Plot of Total Bill by Time and Smoker')
plt.show()

title: Bar Plots
content:
Bar Plots with Estimators

Aggregating Categorical Data

Unlike histograms, standard bar plots in Seaborn show the estimator (mean, by default) of continuous values corresponding to each category, taking into account confidence intervals.

Example

Bar Plot Example
import seaborn as sns
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np

titanic = sns.load_dataset("titanic")

# Using standard deviation as custom estimator
sns.barplot(data=titanic, x="class", y="fare", hue="survived", estimator=np.median)
plt.title('Median Fare by Class and Survival')
plt.show()

title: FacetGrids
content:
FacetGrids Customization

Building Custom Multi-plot Grids

FacetGrids allow you to customize complex multi-plot figures. You initialize them with a dataset, then map a plotting function to each facet.

Example

FacetGrid Example
import seaborn as sns
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

tips = sns.load_dataset("tips")

g = sns.FacetGrid(tips, col="time", row="smoker", margin_titles=True)
g.map(sns.scatterplot, "total_bill", "tip", color="#334488")
g.set_axis_labels("Total Bill ($)", "Tip ($)")
g.set_titles(col_template="{col_name} time", row_template="{row_name} smoker")
plt.show()

title: Advanced Palettes
content:
Advanced Color Palettes

Customizing Colors in Seaborn

Choosing the right color palette is critical. Seaborn includes different types of palettes: qualitative (categorical), sequential (numeric progression), and diverging (numeric with center point).

Example

Palette Example
import seaborn as sns
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np

data = np.random.normal(size=(20, 6)) + np.arange(6) / 2

# Creating and applying a custom palette
custom_palette = sns.color_palette("coolwarm", as_cmap=False, n_colors=6)
sns.boxplot(data=data, palette=custom_palette)
plt.title('Boxplots with Coolwarm Palette')
plt.show()

title: Annotations
content:
Adding Annotations

Enhancing Plots with Text

While Seaborn focuses on high-level APIs to construct the plots, you can still use Pyplot's low-level methods (like `ax.text` or `ax.annotate`) on Seaborn figure axes to add custom text annotations.

Example

Annotation Example
import seaborn as sns
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

tips = sns.load_dataset("tips")
ax = sns.scatterplot(data=tips, x="total_bill", y="tip")

# Annotate the point with the highest tip
max_tip_idx = tips["tip"].idxmax()
max_tip_val = tips.loc[max_tip_idx]

ax.annotate(
    "Highest Tip!",
    xy=(max_tip_val["total_bill"], max_tip_val["tip"]),
    xytext=(max_tip_val["total_bill"]-10, max_tip_val["tip"]+2),
    arrowprops=dict(facecolor='black', arrowstyle="->")
)
plt.show()

title: Course Summary
content:
Seaborn Course Summary 🎉

You Did It! 🎉

You have successfully completed the expanded 20-topic Seaborn course! You can now create stunning and highly informative statistical visualizations with ease.

  • Fundamentals: Datasets, Relational Plots, Distribution Plots.
  • Advanced Distribution: KDE Plots, ECDF Plots, Rug Plots.
  • Categorical Dive: Violin, Swarm, Strip, Point, and Bar Plots.
  • Multi-Dimensional: Matrix Plots, Multi-plot grids (Pairplot, Jointplot) and FacetGrids.
  • Aesthetics: Styling, Themes, Advanced Color Palettes, and custom Annotations.

Your data visualization toolkit is now fully loaded! Feel free to practice these techniques with your own datasets.
